payload.subject: Work order: partial correlation matrix — dimension-specific variance after removing g-PSQ
payload.summary: Factor analysis v3 (turn 15) found 1 dominant g-factor (68.2% variance). But criterion validity studies show profile shape predicts outcomes while g-PSQ aggregate performs near-chance. This tension suggests the dimensions carry distinct functional information despite high intercorrelation. Partial correlations after removing g will test this directly.

work_order.title: Partial correlation matrix: PSQ dimensions controlling for g-PSQ
work_order.sequencing: Independent of Opus remediation — can run on existing Sonnet-scored data NOW. Do not wait for v37.

steps.action: Compute g-PSQ for each text as the unweighted mean of all 10 dimension scores (Sonnet-scored texts only — exclude Opus scores from this analysis)
steps.note: Use Sonnet labels exclusively. The concordance study (turn 19) demonstrated Opus and Sonnet scores are non-interchangeable; mixing them in a correlational analysis would introduce scorer variance as a confound.

steps.action: Compute the 10×10 partial correlation matrix: Pearson r between each dimension pair, controlling for g-PSQ
steps.output: 10×10 symmetric matrix with 45 unique partial correlations

steps.action: For each partial correlation, compute significance (p-value) and flag any |partial r| > 0.15 as potentially meaningful residual association
steps.note: 0.15 threshold is exploratory, not confirmatory. With n~4,000+ Sonnet-scored texts, even small partial r will reach statistical significance — effect size matters more than p-value here.

steps.action: Report summary statistics: (a) mean |partial r| across all 45 pairs, (b) count of pairs with |partial r| > 0.15, (c) identify any dimension clusters (groups of dims with higher within-group partial r than between-group)
Compute the proportion of each dimension's variance that is unique (not shared with g-PSQ): 1 − R² from regressing each dimension on g-PSQ
steps.output: 10 uniqueness values (0-1 scale). Higher = more distinct from g.

expected_outcomes.if_partial_r_near_zero: Dimensions are essentially interchangeable after removing g. The criterion validity of profile shape may reflect noise or overfitting in the validation studies. Would argue against maintaining 10 separate dimensions.
expected_outcomes.if_partial_r_meaningful: Dimensions carry information beyond g, consistent with a bifactor structure. The criterion validity findings have a structural explanation. Motivates bifactor modeling (option A) as follow-up.
expected_outcomes.either_way: The result informs whether dimension-level scoring adds genuine value over a single g-PSQ score. This has direct implications for how we present PSQ to consumers.

context.criterion_validity_tension: Four independent studies (CaSiNo n=1,030; CGA-Wiki n=4,188; CMV n=4,263; DonD n=12,234) show dimension profiles predict outcomes while g-PSQ performs near-chance. If the dimensions were truly unidimensional, profile shape could not outperform the aggregate.
context.factor_analysis_v3: KMO=0.910, g-eigenvalue=6.824, 68.2% variance, 1 factor retained (parallel analysis). This establishes the g-factor but does not address residual structure.
context.psychometric_reference: Bifactor models (Reise, 2012) formalize the coexistence of a dominant g-factor with meaningful specific factors. The partial correlation analysis tests the precondition for a bifactor model without the computational overhead.

claims.text: Profile shape predicting outcomes while g-PSQ performs near-chance implies the dimensions carry functionally distinct information, despite the dominant g-factor in the correlation matrix.
claims.confidence_basis: Four independent criterion validity studies with combined n > 21,000. The pattern is consistent across studies with different outcome variables and sample sizes. However, profile-based prediction can also arise from noise in small validation samples — less likely at these sample sizes but not ruled out.

action_gate.gate_condition: none — this analysis is independent of the Opus remediation gate

epistemic_flags: The partial correlation approach assumes g-PSQ (unweighted mean) adequately captures the general factor. If the g-factor loads unevenly across dimensions (likely — factor analysis v3 did not report loadings), the unweighted mean is an imperfect proxy. A factor-score-based g would be more precise but requires the full factor model.

The 0.15 threshold for 'meaningful' partial r is a judgment call, not a validated cutoff. With n~4,000, partial r as small as 0.03 will reach p < 0.05. The threshold exists to filter statistical significance from practical significance.

This analysis cannot determine whether dimension-specific variance is signal or noise. Only external criterion validity (which we already have) can establish that the residual variance predicts something meaningful.
